title: Prolly Tree, WAL Buffer, and Actor-Per-Shard Foundation
intention: Haematite's core is a storage engine where concurrency is free and branching is structural. When this cluster is complete, a developer can open a haematite database, write to it from hundreds of concurrent actors without any locking discipline, fork it instantly by sharing a hash, and merge two forks by walking only the nodes that differ. The experience should feel like working with an in-memory data structure that happens to be durable — no connection pools, no transaction coordinators, no lock managers. The content-addressed prolly tree is what makes branching zero-friction; the actor-per-shard model is what makes concurrency zero-contention; the WAL buffer is what makes writes fast despite the content-addressing overhead. All three are load-bearing.
problem:
Every embedded key-value store (SQLite, LMDB, RocksDB, sled) serialises writes behind a lock. The moment you need concurrent writers, you pay for lock contention, write-ahead log coordination, and reader-writer scheduling. Branching (forking a database for testing, debugging, or speculative work) is either impossible or implemented as a full copy. Multi-node sync requires replication logs and primary-replica coordination. These are structural limitations of the B-tree and LSM architectures these databases use — not implementation bugs, but consequences of mutable, order-dependent data structures.

Aion needs a storage engine that supports multiple concurrent workflow writers (one per active workflow), instant fork for what-if debugging, and multi-node sync for distribution. The messaging bus needs a persistent backing store for durable channels and conversations. Both need these properties without operational complexity — no connection strings, no replica management, no lock tuning.
solution:
The core cluster delivers three interlocking components:

1. Content-Addressed Prolly Tree

A probabilistic B-tree where node boundaries are determined by content hashing (BLAKE3). The key property: history-independence. The same set of key-value pairs always produces the same tree, regardless of insertion order. Every node is identified by the BLAKE3 hash of its contents.

Node structure: each node is either a leaf (containing sorted key-value pairs) or an internal node (containing sorted key-childHash pairs). Node boundaries are determined by a rolling hash over the key stream: when the hash meets a threshold, a boundary is placed. The threshold controls the expected node size (target: 4KB aligned with OS page size).

Operations: get traverses root to leaf by key comparison. put inserts into the appropriate leaf; if the leaf splits (rolling hash boundary shifts), the parent is updated, potentially cascading to root. Every modified node gets a new hash. Unmodified subtrees keep their existing hashes — this is what makes fork cheap (shared nodes) and merge fast (skip identical subtrees by hash comparison).

The tree root hash is the database identity at a point in time. Two databases with the same root hash contain exactly the same data.

2. WAL Buffer

A per-shard write-ahead log that accumulates mutations before committing them to the prolly tree. The write path: caller sends a put/delete message to the shard actor; the actor appends to its in-memory WAL buffer and acknowledges immediately. The WAL is durably written to disk (sequential append, fast). Periodically — or on explicit commit — the buffer flushes to the prolly tree as a single batch, producing one new root hash.

This amortises the content-addressing overhead: N buffered writes produce one path-to-root rewrite, not N. For Aion's workload (batch event appends), this aligns perfectly — the recorder already batches.

Reads check the buffer first (in-memory, fast), then the tree. Buffer entries shadow tree entries for the same key.

Crash recovery: on restart, the shard actor replays its durable WAL from the last committed root hash forward, reconstructing the buffer state.

3. Actor-Per-Shard

The keyspace is partitioned into N shards (fixed at database creation; see ADR-004). Each shard is a supervised beamr process that owns its prolly tree and WAL buffer. The shard actor is the single writer for its key range — no locks, no contention, no shared mutable state.

Shard routing: shard(K) = hash(K) % N. A router actor maintains the shard table and forwards requests. Clients can cache the routing table for direct sends.

Supervision: each shard actor is supervised. Crash leads to automatic restart with WAL replay. Other shards are unaffected. The supervision tree mirrors beamr's existing OTP-style supervision.

Read replicas: a shard can spawn read-replica processes that trail the primary by subscribing to committed root hashes. Replicas serve reads from their (slightly stale) tree snapshot. This provides read scaling without write contention.

principles.text: Content is identity. A node's hash is derived from its contents, never assigned. Two nodes with the same content have the same hash. This is the foundation of fork, merge, sync, and caching.

The shard actor is the single writer. No operation on a shard's data happens outside that shard's actor. All mutations arrive as messages. This eliminates locks and makes the concurrency model trivially correct.
Writes are buffered, commits are atomic. The WAL buffer is the write hot path; the tree commit is the durability boundary. A commit produces exactly one new root hash from all buffered writes.
Unmodified subtrees are free. Fork, merge, and sync skip subtrees whose root hash matches. The cost of any structural operation is proportional to the differences, not the total data.
The API hides the representation. Callers interact with get/put/delete/range/append/commit. Whether the shard uses a prolly tree, a B+ tree, or a filing cabinet is invisible at the API boundary.
No silent defaults. Every configuration (shard count, commit policy, compression) is explicit. An unconfigured database is an error, not a database with surprising behaviour.

goals: A haematite database can be opened, written to from 256 concurrent shard actors, and read from with zero lock contention.
put followed by get returns the written value within the same shard actor, both from buffer and after commit.
commit produces a deterministic root hash: the same sequence of puts to an empty database always produces the same hash.
Two databases forked from the same root hash and independently mutated can be compared by walking only the differing subtrees.
Crash recovery replays the WAL from the last committed root and reconstructs buffer state without data loss.
range queries return keys in sorted order, merging buffer entries with tree entries correctly.

non_goals.text: Dynamic resharding (changing shard count after creation)
non_goals.reason: Deferred to a future phase per ADR-004. Fixed shard count is sufficient for single-node deployments.
Scan with predicate covers Aion's search-attribute queries. Full secondary indexes deferred until real query patterns are understood.
Covered by the distribution cluster (RM-007). The core cluster provides the content-addressed structure that distribution builds on.
Covered by the wasm cluster (RM-010). The core cluster targets native Rust only.
Covered by the branching cluster (RM-004). The core cluster provides the content-addressed tree that branching depends on, including tree diff (CORE-003).
The single-writer-per-shard model provides serialisability within a shard. Cross-shard transactions are out of scope for the initial release.

structure.note: Node types: leaf and internal, serialisation, BLAKE3 hashing
Rolling hash boundary detection for node splitting
Tree traversal cursor for get/range operations
Tree mutation: insert, delete, node split/merge, path-to-root rewrite
Structural diff between two trees by root hash comparison
In-memory node store (HashMap<Hash, Node>) for testing
On-disk node store with zstd compression
In-memory WAL buffer with sorted mutation log
Shard actor: owns tree + WAL buffer, handles get/put/delete/commit messages
Database handle: opens/creates databases, manages shard actors, exposes public API
Crate manifest with blake3, zstd, beamr dependencies

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
The prolly tree root hash for a given key-value set must be deterministic and history-independent: the same keys and values always produce the same root hash regardless of insertion order.
WAL buffer reads must shadow tree reads: a key written to the buffer but not yet committed must be returned by get, not the stale tree value.
Shard actor crash must not affect other shards. Supervision restarts only the failed shard.
Batch append (multiple key-value pairs in one call) must produce exactly one tree commit, not N individual commits.
